structure_blocks.text: Equipment and vessel requirements

structure_blocks.heading: Trade agreement applicability
structure_blocks.text: Trade agreement applicability

structure_blocks.heading: Trade agreement applicability
structure_blocks.text: When determining if a procurement falls under a trade agreement you must consider:

structure_blocks.heading: Trade agreement applicability
structure_blocks.text: its value

structure_blocks.heading: Trade agreement applicability
structure_blocks.text: the requesting entity

structure_blocks.heading: Trade agreement applicability
structure_blocks.text: the type of requirement

structure_blocks.heading: Trade agreement applicability
structure_blocks.text: applicable exclusions or exceptions

structure_blocks.heading: Trade agreement applicability
structure_blocks.text: set-asides

structure_blocks.heading: Trade agreement applicability
structure_blocks.text: Indigenous Peoples

structure_blocks.heading: Trade agreement applicability
structure_blocks.text: For complex procurements, determining coverage can be challenging . For example, a procurement may involve various clients and product and service categories with some being covered and others not. You should contact the PSPC trade agreement unit for guidance.

structure_blocks.heading: Procurement value
structure_blocks.text: Procurement value

structure_blocks.heading: Procurement value
structure_blocks.text: You must begin by estimating the procurement’s value in Canadian dollars, including options and applicable taxes. If the value is equal to or greater than the thresholds defined in the applicable Treasury Board Contracting Policy Notice , then the procurement might be subject to trade agreements. These thresholds are updated by the TB Secretariat every two years.

structure_blocks.heading: Valuation rules and thresholds
structure_blocks.text: Valuation rules and thresholds

structure_blocks.heading: Valuation rules and thresholds
structure_blocks.text: You must follow the rules set out in the Government Procurement Chapter of each trade agreement, for example:

structure_blocks.heading: Valuation rules and thresholds
structure_blocks.text: CFTA : Government Procurement Chapter, “Valuation” (PDF)

structure_blocks.heading: Valuation rules and thresholds
structure_blocks.text: WTO-AGP : Article II Scope and Coverage

structure_blocks.heading: Valuation rules and thresholds
structure_blocks.text: CETA : Chapter 19 , Article 19.2 – Scope and Coverage

structure_blocks.heading: Valuation rules and thresholds
structure_blocks.text: Comprehensive and Progressive Agreement for Trans-Pacific Partnership (CPTPP): Chapter 15 , Article 15.2: Scope

structure_blocks.heading: Valuation rules and thresholds
structure_blocks.text: You must not divide a procurement, or select or use a particular valuation method, as a tactic to exclude procurements from trade agreement coverage.

structure_blocks.heading: Lowest thresholds
structure_blocks.text: Lowest thresholds

structure_blocks.heading: Lowest thresholds
structure_blocks.text: The Canada–Korea Free Trade Agreement (CKFTA) currently provides the lowest threshold for services at $100,000. When the procedural obligations of the WTO-AGP are met, CKFTA obligations will also be met. Therefore, you must ensure compliance with WTO-AGP for all service procurements valued at or above $100,000 that are covered by the CKFTA . The determination of whether or not a procurement is covered by the CKFTA must still be made pursuant to the Market Access Schedules of the Government Procurement Chapter of that agreement.

structure_blocks.heading: Contracting without trade agreement obligations
structure_blocks.text: Contracting without trade agreement obligations

structure_blocks.heading: Contracting without trade agreement obligations
structure_blocks.text: Tender and contract provisions must be included for procurements with an uncertain maximum value likely to fall below applicable trade agreement thresholds, and where you wish to proceed without following trade agreement obligations. These tender and contract provisions must be included in the solicitation of offers and resulting contract(s) to limit Canada's total potential financial liability to less than these thresholds. Conversely, if the procurement has an uncertain maximum value likely to meet or exceed applicable trade agreement thresholds, you must treat it as covered by the applicable trade agreements, unless explicitly excluded.

structure_blocks.heading: Coverage by entity
structure_blocks.text: Coverage by entity

structure_blocks.heading: Coverage by entity
structure_blocks.text: You must determine if the client is covered by looking at the applicable trade agreement’s Market Access Schedule of Canada found in the annexes to the Government Procurement chapter.

structure_blocks.heading: Coverage by entity
structure_blocks.text: For instance:

structure_blocks.heading: Coverage by entity
structure_blocks.text: all federal departments and agencies are subject to the CFTA procurement obligations, save for those listed as excepted in the Schedule of Canada in the Party-Specific Exceptions Annex to the Government Procurement Chapter

structure_blocks.heading: Coverage by entity
structure_blocks.text: federal government entities covered by the international trade agreements are generally indicated in the first Annex or Section of Canada's Market Access Schedule, for examples, see:

structure_blocks.heading: Coverage by entity
structure_blocks.text: WTO-AGP : Annex 1 – Central Government Entities

structure_blocks.heading: Coverage by entity
structure_blocks.text: CETA : Annex 19-1

structure_blocks.heading: Coverage by entity
structure_blocks.text: CPTPP : Annex 15-A , Section A

structure_blocks.heading: Coverage by entity
structure_blocks.text: all crown corporations are subject to the CFTA procurement obligations, save for those listed as excepted in the Schedule of Canada in the Party-Specific Exceptions Annex to the Government Procurement Chapter

structure_blocks.heading: Coverage by entity
structure_blocks.text: crown corporations covered by the international trade agreements are generally indicated in the third Annex or Section of Canada's Market Access Schedule, for examples, see:

structure_blocks.heading: Coverage by entity
structure_blocks.text: WTO-AGP : Annex 3 – Other Entities

structure_blocks.heading: Coverage by entity
structure_blocks.text: CETA : Annex 19-3

structure_blocks.heading: Coverage by entity
structure_blocks.text: CPTPP : Annex 15-A , Section C

structure_blocks.heading: Coverage of procurement requirements
structure_blocks.text: Coverage of procurement requirements

structure_blocks.heading: Coverage of procurement requirements
structure_blocks.text: You must determine whether the requirement is covered by a trade agreement. Coverage specifics for goods, services and construction are generally found in fourth, fifth and sixth Annexes or Sections of Canada’s Market Access Schedule. For CFTA , the requirements that are not covered are specified in the Non-Application provision of the Government Procurement Chapter or the Schedule of Canada in the Party-Specific Exceptions Annex to the Government Procurement Chapter.

structure_blocks.heading: Coverage of procurement requirements
structure_blocks.text: Goods:

structure_blocks.heading: Coverage of procurement requirements
structure_blocks.text: all goods are covered by the CFTA unless specified otherwise

structure_blocks.heading: Coverage of procurement requirements
structure_blocks.text: in general, all goods are covered by international trade agreements; however, for the Department of National Defence, the Royal Canadian Mounted Police and the Canadian Coast Guard, only certain goods are covered, for examples, see:

structure_blocks.heading: Coverage of procurement requirements
structure_blocks.text: WTO-AGP : Annex 4 – Goods

structure_blocks.heading: Coverage of procurement requirements
structure_blocks.text: CETA : Annex 19-4

structure_blocks.heading: Coverage of procurement requirements
structure_blocks.text: CPTPP : Annex 15-A , Section D

structure_blocks.heading: Coverage of procurement requirements
structure_blocks.text: Canada-Korea Free Trade Agreement: Chapter 14 , Annex 14-B

structure_blocks.heading: Coverage of procurement requirements
structure_blocks.text: Services:

structure_blocks.heading: Coverage of procurement requirements
structure_blocks.text: all services are covered by the CFTA unless otherwise specified

structure_blocks.heading: Coverage of procurement requirements
structure_blocks.text: coverage of services varies between international trade agreements some typically cover only expressly listed services, while others cover all services except those expressly listed, for examples, see:

structure_blocks.heading: Coverage of procurement requirements
structure_blocks.text: WTO-AGP : Annex 5 - Services

structure_blocks.heading: Coverage of procurement requirements
structure_blocks.text: CETA : Annex 19-5

structure_blocks.heading: Coverage of procurement requirements
structure_blocks.text: CPTPP : Annex 15-A , Section E

structure_blocks.heading: Coverage of procurement requirements
structure_blocks.text: CKFTA : Chapter 14 , Annex 14-C

structure_blocks.heading: Coverage of procurement requirements
structure_blocks.text: CCFTA : Annex K bis-01.1-4

structure_blocks.heading: Coverage of procurement requirements
structure_blocks.text: for the DND , the RCMP and the Canadian Coast Guard, services related to goods are only covered if the goods they relate to are covered

structure_blocks.heading: Coverage of procurement requirements
structure_blocks.text: Construction Services:

structure_blocks.heading: Coverage of procurement requirements
structure_blocks.text: all construction services, including dredging services and construction services procured by or on behalf of the Department of Transport, are covered by the CFTA unless otherwise specified

structure_blocks.heading: Coverage of procurement requirements
structure_blocks.text: in general, those identified in Division 51 of the United Nations Central Product Classification (CPC) are covered by international trade agreements, except for:

structure_blocks.heading: Coverage of procurement requirements
structure_blocks.text: construction services procured by or on behalf of the Department of Transport

structure_blocks.heading: Coverage of procurement requirements
structure_blocks.text: dredging services are only covered by CETA and the Canada-United Kingdom Trade Continuity Agreement (Canada-UK TCA)

structure_blocks.heading: Coverage of procurement requirements
structure_blocks.text: for examples, see:

structure_blocks.heading: Coverage of procurement requirements
structure_blocks.text: WTO-AGP : Annex 6 – Construction Services

structure_blocks.heading: Coverage of procurement requirements
structure_blocks.text: CETA : Annex 19-6

structure_blocks.heading: Coverage of procurement requirements
structure_blocks.text: CPTPP : Annex 15-A , Section F

structure_blocks.heading: Addressing exclusions and exceptions
structure_blocks.text: Addressing exclusions and exceptions

structure_blocks.heading: Addressing exclusions and exceptions
structure_blocks.text: You must determine if any exclusions from trade agreement obligations apply to the procurement by reviewing the list of exclusions found in Canada’s Market Access Schedule. Trade agreements do not apply to any procurement where an express exclusion exists.

structure_blocks.heading: Addressing exclusions and exceptions
structure_blocks.text: For examples, see:

structure_blocks.heading: Addressing exclusions and exceptions
structure_blocks.text: CFTA : General Exceptions Chapter

structure_blocks.heading: Addressing exclusions and exceptions
structure_blocks.text: WTO-AGP : Annex 7 – General Notes

structure_blocks.heading: Addressing exclusions and exceptions
structure_blocks.text: CETA : Annex 19-7

structure_blocks.heading: Addressing exclusions and exceptions
structure_blocks.text: CPTPP : Annex 15-A , Section G

structure_blocks.heading: Addressing exclusions and exceptions
structure_blocks.text: You must decide if any exceptions, such as a national security exception (NSE) or the general exception for human life and safety, will be invoked for the requirement. If you invoke an exception, this could exempt some or all aspects of the procurement from certain trade agreement obligations. To mitigate risk, you should follow the trade agreement obligations as much as possible. Only deviate where necessary to achieve the purposes of the exception or set-aside, or to implement the measures for Indigenous Peoples or businesses.

structure_blocks.heading: Addressing exclusions and exceptions
structure_blocks.text: For the list of general exceptions refer to the Security and General Exceptions Article of the Government Procurement Chapter. Some trade agreements, such as the CPTPP , also include exceptions in a specific “Exceptions” Chapter. For examples, see:

structure_blocks.heading: Addressing exclusions and exceptions
structure_blocks.text: CFTA : General Exceptions Chapter

structure_blocks.heading: Addressing exclusions and exceptions
structure_blocks.text: WTO-AGP : Article III – Security and General Exceptions

structure_blocks.heading: Addressing exclusions and exceptions
structure_blocks.text: CETA : Chapter 19 , Article 19.3: Security and General Exceptions

structure_blocks.heading: Addressing exclusions and exceptions
structure_blocks.text: CPTPP : Chapter 15 , Article 15.3: Exceptions and Chapter 29 , Article 29.2: Security Exceptions

structure_blocks.heading: Set-asides
structure_blocks.text: Set-asides

structure_blocks.heading: Set-asides
structure_blocks.text: You should decide if any set-asides, such as the set-aside for minority businesses, will be applied to the procurement. You may apply the set-asides to the entire procurement or only part of it. This means that all or some aspects may not be subject to certain trade agreement obligations.

structure_blocks.heading: Set-asides
structure_blocks.text: You may refer to Canada's Market Access Schedule for Canada’s set-asides. For examples, see:

structure_blocks.heading: Set-asides
structure_blocks.text: CFTA : Government Procurement Chapter, “Set-asides”

structure_blocks.heading: Set-asides
structure_blocks.text: WTO-AGP : Annex 7 – General Notes

structure_blocks.heading: Set-asides
structure_blocks.text: CETA : Annex 19-7

structure_blocks.heading: Set-asides
structure_blocks.text: CPTPP : Annex 15-A , Section G

structure_blocks.heading: Indigenous Peoples
structure_blocks.text: Indigenous Peoples

structure_blocks.heading: Indigenous Peoples
structure_blocks.text: All trade agreements contain mechanisms that allow Canada to add measures, including but not limited to set-asides, for the benefit of Indigenous Peoples or businesses in a procurement. This includes procurement obligations under Modern Treaties (Comprehensive Land Claims Agreements ).

structure_blocks.heading: Indigenous Peoples
structure_blocks.text: If a measure is adopted or maintained for Indigenous Peoples in a procurement that is otherwise covered by trade agreements, some or all aspects of it may not be subject to trade agreement obligations.

structure_blocks.heading: Coverage of dredging services under the trade agreements
heading_path: Coverage of dredging services under the trade agreements
structure_blocks.text: Coverage of dredging services under the trade agreements

structure_blocks.heading: Coverage of dredging services under the trade agreements
heading_path: Coverage of dredging services under the trade agreements
structure_blocks.text: The coverage of dredging services is described below.

structure_blocks.heading: Applicable trade agreements
heading_path: Coverage of dredging services under the trade agreements
structure_blocks.text: Applicable trade agreements

structure_blocks.heading: Applicable trade agreements
heading_path: Coverage of dredging services under the trade agreements
structure_blocks.text: The CETA and the Canada-UK TCA are the only international trade agreements that cover dredging services (see CETA Annex 19-6 - Construction services and Canada-UK TCA Article I. As such, when CETA and the Canada-UK TCA apply, in addition to dredging equipment of Canadian make and manufacture, dredging equipment and vessels of European Union (EU) member state and United Kingdom (UK) make and manufacture are permitted on the work, whether in dredging procurements or construction services procurements with dredging as a component.

structure_blocks.heading: Conditions for coverage
heading_path: Coverage of dredging services under the trade agreements
structure_blocks.text: Conditions for coverage

structure_blocks.heading: Conditions for coverage
heading_path: Coverage of dredging services under the trade agreements
structure_blocks.text: Dredging services incidental to construction services contracts are only covered for central government entities. The CETA and Canada-UK TCA construction services thresholds for central government entities apply.

structure_blocks.heading: Conditions for coverage
heading_path: Coverage of dredging services under the trade agreements
structure_blocks.text: For dredging procurements under CETA and the Canada-UK TCA , or dredging services incidental to CETA and Canada-UK TCA covered construction services contracts, one of the following requirements apply:

structure_blocks.heading: Conditions for coverage
heading_path: Coverage of dredging services under the trade agreements
structure_blocks.text: equipment and vessels must be of Canadian, EU or UK make or manufacture

structure_blocks.heading: Conditions for coverage
heading_path: Coverage of dredging services under the trade agreements
structure_blocks.text: equipment and vessels must have been predominantly modified in Canada, the EU or the UK and has been owned by a person located in Canada, the EU or the UK for at least a year before offer submission

structure_blocks.heading: Vessel registration and licensing
heading_path: Coverage of dredging services under the trade agreements
structure_blocks.text: Vessel registration and licensing

structure_blocks.heading: Vessel registration and licensing
heading_path: Coverage of dredging services under the trade agreements
structure_blocks.text: The following requirements apply:

structure_blocks.heading: Vessel registration and licensing
heading_path: Coverage of dredging services under the trade agreements
structure_blocks.text: vessels used must be registered in Canada, the UK or an EU Member State

structure_blocks.heading: Vessel registration and licensing
heading_path: Coverage of dredging services under the trade agreements
structure_blocks.text: if not registered in Canada, the vessel must have a temporary licence under the Coasting Trade Act , granted based on specific non-discretionary requirements

structure_blocks.heading: Vessel registration and licensing
heading_path: Coverage of dredging services under the trade agreements
structure_blocks.text: the usual requirement, that a temporary licence is only issued if no Canadian vessel is available, does not apply in these cases

structure_blocks.heading: Applicability of trade agreements to standing offers and supply arrangements
heading_path: Applicability of trade agreements to standing offers and supply arrangements
structure_blocks.text: Applicability of trade agreements to standing offers and supply arrangements

structure_blocks.heading: Applicability of trade agreements to standing offers and supply arrangements
heading_path: Applicability of trade agreements to standing offers and supply arrangements
structure_blocks.text: You must assess the applicability of the trade agreements related to standing offers and supply arrangements. In your assessment, you must consider the following three factors:

structure_blocks.heading: Applicability of trade agreements to standing offers and supply arrangements
heading_path: Applicability of trade agreements to standing offers and supply arrangements
structure_blocks.text: whether the department for which the standing offer or supply arrangement is intended is covered under the trade agreements

structure_blocks.heading: Applicability of trade agreements to standing offers and supply arrangements
heading_path: Applicability of trade agreements to standing offers and supply arrangements
structure_blocks.text: whether the good or service categories involved are subject to the trade agreements

structure_blocks.heading: Applicability of trade agreements to standing offers and supply arrangements
heading_path: Applicability of trade agreements to standing offers and supply arrangements
structure_blocks.text: whether the total estimated value of all the call-ups (contracts) against an standing offer or all contracts under an supply arrangement meets or exceeds the trade agreement thresholds

structure_blocks.heading: Applicability of trade agreements to standing offers and supply arrangements
heading_path: Applicability of trade agreements to standing offers and supply arrangements
structure_blocks.text: If trade agreements are applicable, solicitations for standing offers and supply arrangements must comply with these agreements.

structure_blocks.heading: Applicability of trade agreements to standing offers and supply arrangements
heading_path: Applicability of trade agreements to standing offers and supply arrangements
structure_blocks.text: For standing offer, individual call-ups are not considered separate procurements under the trade agreements. They can proceed without following trade agreement procedures, provided that the procedures specified in the standing offer for making call-ups are followed.

structure_blocks.heading: Applicability of trade agreements to standing offers and supply arrangements
heading_path: Applicability of trade agreements to standing offers and supply arrangements
structure_blocks.text: For supply arrangements, each individual contract cannot be made under the supply arrangements without having considered trade agreement applicability and procedural obligations, whether at the RFSA stage or at the point of placing an individual contract. For more information refer to Request for supply arrangement .

structure_blocks.heading: Invoking a national security exception
structure_blocks.text: Invoking a national security exception

structure_blocks.heading: Invoking a national security exception
structure_blocks.text: The section below provides guidance on how to proceed when invoking a national security exception to exclude a procurement from one or more trade agreements.

structure_blocks.heading: Client responsibilities
structure_blocks.text: Client responsibilities

structure_blocks.heading: Client responsibilities
structure_blocks.text: When a client wants to invoke an NSE to exclude the procurement from a trade agreement on a PSPC Acquisitions Program (AP) requisition they must prepare a letter of request from the responsible Assistant Deputy Minister (ADM) or equivalent at their department to the ADM/AP for approval, regardless of dollar value, where PSPC/AP is the contracting authority.

structure_blocks.heading: Client responsibilities
structure_blocks.text: This letter must explain the nature of the proposed procurement and, depending on the applicable trade agreement(s), how it relates to Canada’s:

structure_blocks.heading: Client responsibilities
structure_blocks.text: “national security interests” or international obligations, the “maintenance of international peace and security,” CFTA (PDF), Article 801: National Security

structure_blocks.heading: Client responsibilities
structure_blocks.text: “essential security interests for the procurement of arms, ammunition or war materials, or to procurement indispensable for national security or for national defence purposes” (see the relevant text in the trade agreements below):

structure_blocks.heading: Client responsibilities
structure_blocks.text: CETA , Article 19.3 (1): Security and general exceptions

structure_blocks.heading: Client responsibilities
structure_blocks.text: WTO-AGP , Article III (1): Security and General Exceptions

structure_blocks.heading: Client responsibilities
structure_blocks.text: CPTPP , Article 29.2: Security Exceptions

structure_blocks.heading: Client responsibilities
structure_blocks.text: Canada-Chile , Article Kbis-16 (1): Exceptions

structure_blocks.heading: Client responsibilities
structure_blocks.text: Canada-Columbia , Article 1402 (1): Security and General Exceptions

structure_blocks.heading: Client responsibilities
structure_blocks.text: Canada-Honduras , Article 17.3 (1): Security and General Exceptions

structure_blocks.heading: Client responsibilities
structure_blocks.text: Canada-Korea , Article 22.2: National Security

structure_blocks.heading: Client responsibilities
structure_blocks.text: Canada-Panama , Article 16.03 (1): Security and General Exceptions

structure_blocks.heading: Client responsibilities
structure_blocks.text: Canada-Peru , Article 1402 (1): Security and General Exceptions

structure_blocks.heading: Client responsibilities
structure_blocks.text: Canada-UK TCA , Article I

structure_blocks.heading: Client responsibilities
structure_blocks.text: Canada-Ukraine (CUFTA) , Article 10.4 (1): Security and General Exceptions

structure_blocks.heading: Client responsibilities
structure_blocks.text: First, the client should submit a draft of the NSE request letter to the PSPC Specialized Services for Procurement Group (SSPG) for review before it is signed by their responsible ADM or equivalent. The PSPC SSPG reviews all NSE requests before seeking PSPC ADM / AP approval. Then, the client submits the request to the PSPC ADM / AP responsible for approving NSE requests.

structure_blocks.heading: Client responsibilities
structure_blocks.text: The ADM reviews the issue of invoking the NSE , not other procurement matters such as methods, plans or contract authority. Once the ADM makes a decision, they will inform the client in writing. For the approval process for NSE , you may refer to Approval authorities for Acquisition Program client's programs only .

structure_blocks.heading: Client responsibilities
structure_blocks.text: Clients should collaborate with you to determine the appropriate procurement method, in parallel with any request for approval of an NSE . Even if an NSE is invoked, it is government policy to compete the requirement, unless exceptions to competitive contracting in the GCRs apply.

structure_blocks.heading: Buyer responsibilities
structure_blocks.text: Buyer responsibilities

structure_blocks.heading: Buyer responsibilities
structure_blocks.text: Once an NSE is invoked and applied to the requirement, you must include a statement in all procurement documents indicating this. Use the statement:

structure_blocks.heading: Buyer responsibilities
structure_blocks.text: "This procurement is subject to a national security exception and is, therefore, excluded from all of the obligations of the trade agreements."

structure_blocks.heading: Buyer responsibilities
structure_blocks.text: You must specify in approval documents the rationale for invoking the NSE and the trade agreements from which the procurement is being excluded.

structure_blocks.heading: Buyer responsibilities
structure_blocks.text: You must keep a copy of the NSE approval letter on file and ensure that all procurement documents reflect the invocation of the NSE . You are to maintain proper documentation as required by the GCRs and other relevant policies. Invoking an NSE does not exempt you from complying with the GCRs and other relevant regulations and policies. This may include posting an NPP or an Advance Contract Award Notice (ACAN) on the Tender opportunities page, unless security requirements prevent such actions.

structure_blocks.heading: Buyer responsibilities
structure_blocks.text: By following these procedures, clients can properly request an NSE , and you can ensure all necessary steps and documentation are in place for compliance. For advice on assisting clients in properly framing and requesting an NSE , and for an NSE letter template, contact the Specialized Services for Procurement Group .

raw_text:
Equipment and vessel requirements

Trade agreement applicability

When determining if a procurement falls under a trade agreement you must consider:

its value

the requesting entity

the type of requirement

applicable exclusions or exceptions

set-asides

Indigenous Peoples

For complex procurements, determining coverage can be challenging . For example, a procurement may involve various clients and product and service categories with some being covered and others not. You should contact the PSPC trade agreement unit for guidance.

Procurement value

You must begin by estimating the procurement’s value in Canadian dollars, including options and applicable taxes. If the value is equal to or greater than the thresholds defined in the applicable Treasury Board Contracting Policy Notice , then the procurement might be subject to trade agreements. These thresholds are updated by the TB Secretariat every two years.

Valuation rules and thresholds

You must follow the rules set out in the Government Procurement Chapter of each trade agreement, for example:

CFTA : Government Procurement Chapter, “Valuation” (PDF)

WTO-AGP : Article II Scope and Coverage

CETA : Chapter 19 , Article 19.2 – Scope and Coverage

Comprehensive and Progressive Agreement for Trans-Pacific Partnership (CPTPP): Chapter 15 , Article 15.2: Scope

You must not divide a procurement, or select or use a particular valuation method, as a tactic to exclude procurements from trade agreement coverage.

Lowest thresholds

The Canada–Korea Free Trade Agreement (CKFTA) currently provides the lowest threshold for services at $100,000. When the procedural obligations of the WTO-AGP are met, CKFTA obligations will also be met. Therefore, you must ensure compliance with WTO-AGP for all service procurements valued at or above $100,000 that are covered by the CKFTA . The determination of whether or not a procurement is covered by the CKFTA must still be made pursuant to the Market Access Schedules of the Government Procurement Chapter of that agreement.

Contracting without trade agreement obligations

Tender and contract provisions must be included for procurements with an uncertain maximum value likely to fall below applicable trade agreement thresholds, and where you wish to proceed without following trade agreement obligations. These tender and contract provisions must be included in the solicitation of offers and resulting contract(s) to limit Canada's total potential financial liability to less than these thresholds. Conversely, if the procurement has an uncertain maximum value likely to meet or exceed applicable trade agreement thresholds, you must treat it as covered by the applicable trade agreements, unless explicitly excluded.

Coverage by entity

You must determine if the client is covered by looking at the applicable trade agreement’s Market Access Schedule of Canada found in the annexes to the Government Procurement chapter.

For instance:

all federal departments and agencies are subject to the CFTA procurement obligations, save for those listed as excepted in the Schedule of Canada in the Party-Specific Exceptions Annex to the Government Procurement Chapter

federal government entities covered by the international trade agreements are generally indicated in the first Annex or Section of Canada's Market Access Schedule, for examples, see:

WTO-AGP : Annex 1 – Central Government Entities

CETA : Annex 19-1

CPTPP : Annex 15-A , Section A

all crown corporations are subject to the CFTA procurement obligations, save for those listed as excepted in the Schedule of Canada in the Party-Specific Exceptions Annex to the Government Procurement Chapter

crown corporations covered by the international trade agreements are generally indicated in the third Annex or Section of Canada's Market Access Schedule, for examples, see:

WTO-AGP : Annex 3 – Other Entities

CETA : Annex 19-3

CPTPP : Annex 15-A , Section C

Coverage of procurement requirements

You must determine whether the requirement is covered by a trade agreement. Coverage specifics for goods, services and construction are generally found in fourth, fifth and sixth Annexes or Sections of Canada’s Market Access Schedule. For CFTA , the requirements that are not covered are specified in the Non-Application provision of the Government Procurement Chapter or the Schedule of Canada in the Party-Specific Exceptions Annex to the Government Procurement Chapter.

Goods:

all goods are covered by the CFTA unless specified otherwise

in general, all goods are covered by international trade agreements; however, for the Department of National Defence, the Royal Canadian Mounted Police and the Canadian Coast Guard, only certain goods are covered, for examples, see:

WTO-AGP : Annex 4 – Goods

CETA : Annex 19-4

CPTPP : Annex 15-A , Section D

Canada-Korea Free Trade Agreement: Chapter 14 , Annex 14-B

Services:

all services are covered by the CFTA unless otherwise specified

coverage of services varies between international trade agreements some typically cover only expressly listed services, while others cover all services except those expressly listed, for examples, see:

WTO-AGP : Annex 5 - Services

CETA : Annex 19-5

CPTPP : Annex 15-A , Section E

CKFTA : Chapter 14 , Annex 14-C

CCFTA : Annex K bis-01.1-4

for the DND , the RCMP and the Canadian Coast Guard, services related to goods are only covered if the goods they relate to are covered

Construction Services:

all construction services, including dredging services and construction services procured by or on behalf of the Department of Transport, are covered by the CFTA unless otherwise specified

in general, those identified in Division 51 of the United Nations Central Product Classification (CPC) are covered by international trade agreements, except for:

construction services procured by or on behalf of the Department of Transport

dredging services are only covered by CETA and the Canada-United Kingdom Trade Continuity Agreement (Canada-UK TCA)

for examples, see:

WTO-AGP : Annex 6 – Construction Services

CETA : Annex 19-6

CPTPP : Annex 15-A , Section F

Addressing exclusions and exceptions

You must determine if any exclusions from trade agreement obligations apply to the procurement by reviewing the list of exclusions found in Canada’s Market Access Schedule. Trade agreements do not apply to any procurement where an express exclusion exists.

For examples, see:

CFTA : General Exceptions Chapter

WTO-AGP : Annex 7 – General Notes

CETA : Annex 19-7

CPTPP : Annex 15-A , Section G

You must decide if any exceptions, such as a national security exception (NSE) or the general exception for human life and safety, will be invoked for the requirement. If you invoke an exception, this could exempt some or all aspects of the procurement from certain trade agreement obligations. To mitigate risk, you should follow the trade agreement obligations as much as possible. Only deviate where necessary to achieve the purposes of the exception or set-aside, or to implement the measures for Indigenous Peoples or businesses.

For the list of general exceptions refer to the Security and General Exceptions Article of the Government Procurement Chapter. Some trade agreements, such as the CPTPP , also include exceptions in a specific “Exceptions” Chapter. For examples, see:

CFTA : General Exceptions Chapter

WTO-AGP : Article III – Security and General Exceptions

CETA : Chapter 19 , Article 19.3: Security and General Exceptions

CPTPP : Chapter 15 , Article 15.3: Exceptions and Chapter 29 , Article 29.2: Security Exceptions

Set-asides

You should decide if any set-asides, such as the set-aside for minority businesses, will be applied to the procurement. You may apply the set-asides to the entire procurement or only part of it. This means that all or some aspects may not be subject to certain trade agreement obligations.

You may refer to Canada's Market Access Schedule for Canada’s set-asides. For examples, see:

CFTA : Government Procurement Chapter, “Set-asides”

WTO-AGP : Annex 7 – General Notes

CETA : Annex 19-7

CPTPP : Annex 15-A , Section G

Indigenous Peoples

All trade agreements contain mechanisms that allow Canada to add measures, including but not limited to set-asides, for the benefit of Indigenous Peoples or businesses in a procurement. This includes procurement obligations under Modern Treaties (Comprehensive Land Claims Agreements ).

If a measure is adopted or maintained for Indigenous Peoples in a procurement that is otherwise covered by trade agreements, some or all aspects of it may not be subject to trade agreement obligations.

Coverage of dredging services under the trade agreements

The coverage of dredging services is described below.

Applicable trade agreements

The CETA and the Canada-UK TCA are the only international trade agreements that cover dredging services (see CETA Annex 19-6 - Construction services and Canada-UK TCA Article I. As such, when CETA and the Canada-UK TCA apply, in addition to dredging equipment of Canadian make and manufacture, dredging equipment and vessels of European Union (EU) member state and United Kingdom (UK) make and manufacture are permitted on the work, whether in dredging procurements or construction services procurements with dredging as a component.

Conditions for coverage

Dredging services incidental to construction services contracts are only covered for central government entities. The CETA and Canada-UK TCA construction services thresholds for central government entities apply.

For dredging procurements under CETA and the Canada-UK TCA , or dredging services incidental to CETA and Canada-UK TCA covered construction services contracts, one of the following requirements apply:

equipment and vessels must be of Canadian, EU or UK make or manufacture

equipment and vessels must have been predominantly modified in Canada, the EU or the UK and has been owned by a person located in Canada, the EU or the UK for at least a year before offer submission

Vessel registration and licensing

The following requirements apply:

vessels used must be registered in Canada, the UK or an EU Member State

if not registered in Canada, the vessel must have a temporary licence under the Coasting Trade Act , granted based on specific non-discretionary requirements

the usual requirement, that a temporary licence is only issued if no Canadian vessel is available, does not apply in these cases

Applicability of trade agreements to standing offers and supply arrangements

You must assess the applicability of the trade agreements related to standing offers and supply arrangements. In your assessment, you must consider the following three factors:

whether the department for which the standing offer or supply arrangement is intended is covered under the trade agreements

whether the good or service categories involved are subject to the trade agreements

whether the total estimated value of all the call-ups (contracts) against an standing offer or all contracts under an supply arrangement meets or exceeds the trade agreement thresholds

If trade agreements are applicable, solicitations for standing offers and supply arrangements must comply with these agreements.

For standing offer, individual call-ups are not considered separate procurements under the trade agreements. They can proceed without following trade agreement procedures, provided that the procedures specified in the standing offer for making call-ups are followed.

For supply arrangements, each individual contract cannot be made under the supply arrangements without having considered trade agreement applicability and procedural obligations, whether at the RFSA stage or at the point of placing an individual contract. For more information refer to Request for supply arrangement .

Invoking a national security exception

The section below provides guidance on how to proceed when invoking a national security exception to exclude a procurement from one or more trade agreements.

Client responsibilities

When a client wants to invoke an NSE to exclude the procurement from a trade agreement on a PSPC Acquisitions Program (AP) requisition they must prepare a letter of request from the responsible Assistant Deputy Minister (ADM) or equivalent at their department to the ADM/AP for approval, regardless of dollar value, where PSPC/AP is the contracting authority.

This letter must explain the nature of the proposed procurement and, depending on the applicable trade agreement(s), how it relates to Canada’s:

“national security interests” or international obligations, the “maintenance of international peace and security,” CFTA (PDF), Article 801: National Security

“essential security interests for the procurement of arms, ammunition or war materials, or to procurement indispensable for national security or for national defence purposes” (see the relevant text in the trade agreements below):

CETA , Article 19.3 (1): Security and general exceptions

WTO-AGP , Article III (1): Security and General Exceptions

CPTPP , Article 29.2: Security Exceptions

Canada-Chile , Article Kbis-16 (1): Exceptions

Canada-Columbia , Article 1402 (1): Security and General Exceptions

Canada-Honduras , Article 17.3 (1): Security and General Exceptions

Canada-Korea , Article 22.2: National Security

Canada-Panama , Article 16.03 (1): Security and General Exceptions

Canada-Peru , Article 1402 (1): Security and General Exceptions

Canada-UK TCA , Article I

Canada-Ukraine (CUFTA) , Article 10.4 (1): Security and General Exceptions

First, the client should submit a draft of the NSE request letter to the PSPC Specialized Services for Procurement Group (SSPG) for review before it is signed by their responsible ADM or equivalent. The PSPC SSPG reviews all NSE requests before seeking PSPC ADM / AP approval. Then, the client submits the request to the PSPC ADM / AP responsible for approving NSE requests.

The ADM reviews the issue of invoking the NSE , not other procurement matters such as methods, plans or contract authority. Once the ADM makes a decision, they will inform the client in writing. For the approval process for NSE , you may refer to Approval authorities for Acquisition Program client's programs only .

Clients should collaborate with you to determine the appropriate procurement method, in parallel with any request for approval of an NSE . Even if an NSE is invoked, it is government policy to compete the requirement, unless exceptions to competitive contracting in the GCRs apply.

Buyer responsibilities

Once an NSE is invoked and applied to the requirement, you must include a statement in all procurement documents indicating this. Use the statement:

"This procurement is subject to a national security exception and is, therefore, excluded from all of the obligations of the trade agreements."

You must specify in approval documents the rationale for invoking the NSE and the trade agreements from which the procurement is being excluded.

You must keep a copy of the NSE approval letter on file and ensure that all procurement documents reflect the invocation of the NSE . You are to maintain proper documentation as required by the GCRs and other relevant policies. Invoking an NSE does not exempt you from complying with the GCRs and other relevant regulations and policies. This may include posting an NPP or an Advance Contract Award Notice (ACAN) on the Tender opportunities page, unless security requirements prevent such actions.

By following these procedures, clients can properly request an NSE , and you can ensure all necessary steps and documentation are in place for compliance. For advice on assisting clients in properly framing and requesting an NSE , and for an NSE letter template, contact the Specialized Services for Procurement Group .
markdown_text:
Equipment and vessel requirements

Trade agreement applicability

When determining if a procurement falls under a trade agreement you must consider:

its value

the requesting entity

the type of requirement

applicable exclusions or exceptions

set-asides

Indigenous Peoples

For complex procurements, determining coverage can be challenging . For example, a procurement may involve various clients and product and service categories with some being covered and others not. You should contact the PSPC trade agreement unit for guidance.

Procurement value

You must begin by estimating the procurement’s value in Canadian dollars, including options and applicable taxes. If the value is equal to or greater than the thresholds defined in the applicable Treasury Board Contracting Policy Notice , then the procurement might be subject to trade agreements. These thresholds are updated by the TB Secretariat every two years.

Valuation rules and thresholds

You must follow the rules set out in the Government Procurement Chapter of each trade agreement, for example:

CFTA : Government Procurement Chapter, “Valuation” (PDF)

WTO-AGP : Article II Scope and Coverage

CETA : Chapter 19 , Article 19.2 – Scope and Coverage

Comprehensive and Progressive Agreement for Trans-Pacific Partnership (CPTPP): Chapter 15 , Article 15.2: Scope

You must not divide a procurement, or select or use a particular valuation method, as a tactic to exclude procurements from trade agreement coverage.

Lowest thresholds

The Canada–Korea Free Trade Agreement (CKFTA) currently provides the lowest threshold for services at $100,000. When the procedural obligations of the WTO-AGP are met, CKFTA obligations will also be met. Therefore, you must ensure compliance with WTO-AGP for all service procurements valued at or above $100,000 that are covered by the CKFTA . The determination of whether or not a procurement is covered by the CKFTA must still be made pursuant to the Market Access Schedules of the Government Procurement Chapter of that agreement.

Contracting without trade agreement obligations

Tender and contract provisions must be included for procurements with an uncertain maximum value likely to fall below applicable trade agreement thresholds, and where you wish to proceed without following trade agreement obligations. These tender and contract provisions must be included in the solicitation of offers and resulting contract(s) to limit Canada's total potential financial liability to less than these thresholds. Conversely, if the procurement has an uncertain maximum value likely to meet or exceed applicable trade agreement thresholds, you must treat it as covered by the applicable trade agreements, unless explicitly excluded.

Coverage by entity

You must determine if the client is covered by looking at the applicable trade agreement’s Market Access Schedule of Canada found in the annexes to the Government Procurement chapter.

For instance:

all federal departments and agencies are subject to the CFTA procurement obligations, save for those listed as excepted in the Schedule of Canada in the Party-Specific Exceptions Annex to the Government Procurement Chapter

federal government entities covered by the international trade agreements are generally indicated in the first Annex or Section of Canada's Market Access Schedule, for examples, see:

WTO-AGP : Annex 1 – Central Government Entities

CETA : Annex 19-1

CPTPP : Annex 15-A , Section A

all crown corporations are subject to the CFTA procurement obligations, save for those listed as excepted in the Schedule of Canada in the Party-Specific Exceptions Annex to the Government Procurement Chapter

crown corporations covered by the international trade agreements are generally indicated in the third Annex or Section of Canada's Market Access Schedule, for examples, see:

WTO-AGP : Annex 3 – Other Entities

CETA : Annex 19-3

CPTPP : Annex 15-A , Section C

Coverage of procurement requirements

You must determine whether the requirement is covered by a trade agreement. Coverage specifics for goods, services and construction are generally found in fourth, fifth and sixth Annexes or Sections of Canada’s Market Access Schedule. For CFTA , the requirements that are not covered are specified in the Non-Application provision of the Government Procurement Chapter or the Schedule of Canada in the Party-Specific Exceptions Annex to the Government Procurement Chapter.

Goods:

all goods are covered by the CFTA unless specified otherwise

in general, all goods are covered by international trade agreements; however, for the Department of National Defence, the Royal Canadian Mounted Police and the Canadian Coast Guard, only certain goods are covered, for examples, see:

WTO-AGP : Annex 4 – Goods

CETA : Annex 19-4

CPTPP : Annex 15-A , Section D

Canada-Korea Free Trade Agreement: Chapter 14 , Annex 14-B

Services:

all services are covered by the CFTA unless otherwise specified

coverage of services varies between international trade agreements some typically cover only expressly listed services, while others cover all services except those expressly listed, for examples, see:

WTO-AGP : Annex 5 - Services

CETA : Annex 19-5

CPTPP : Annex 15-A , Section E

CKFTA : Chapter 14 , Annex 14-C

CCFTA : Annex K bis-01.1-4

for the DND , the RCMP and the Canadian Coast Guard, services related to goods are only covered if the goods they relate to are covered

Construction Services:

all construction services, including dredging services and construction services procured by or on behalf of the Department of Transport, are covered by the CFTA unless otherwise specified

in general, those identified in Division 51 of the United Nations Central Product Classification (CPC) are covered by international trade agreements, except for:

construction services procured by or on behalf of the Department of Transport

dredging services are only covered by CETA and the Canada-United Kingdom Trade Continuity Agreement (Canada-UK TCA)

for examples, see:

WTO-AGP : Annex 6 – Construction Services

CETA : Annex 19-6

CPTPP : Annex 15-A , Section F

Addressing exclusions and exceptions

You must determine if any exclusions from trade agreement obligations apply to the procurement by reviewing the list of exclusions found in Canada’s Market Access Schedule. Trade agreements do not apply to any procurement where an express exclusion exists.

For examples, see:

CFTA : General Exceptions Chapter

WTO-AGP : Annex 7 – General Notes

CETA : Annex 19-7

CPTPP : Annex 15-A , Section G

You must decide if any exceptions, such as a national security exception (NSE) or the general exception for human life and safety, will be invoked for the requirement. If you invoke an exception, this could exempt some or all aspects of the procurement from certain trade agreement obligations. To mitigate risk, you should follow the trade agreement obligations as much as possible. Only deviate where necessary to achieve the purposes of the exception or set-aside, or to implement the measures for Indigenous Peoples or businesses.

For the list of general exceptions refer to the Security and General Exceptions Article of the Government Procurement Chapter. Some trade agreements, such as the CPTPP , also include exceptions in a specific “Exceptions” Chapter. For examples, see:

CFTA : General Exceptions Chapter

WTO-AGP : Article III – Security and General Exceptions

CETA : Chapter 19 , Article 19.3: Security and General Exceptions

CPTPP : Chapter 15 , Article 15.3: Exceptions and Chapter 29 , Article 29.2: Security Exceptions

Set-asides

You should decide if any set-asides, such as the set-aside for minority businesses, will be applied to the procurement. You may apply the set-asides to the entire procurement or only part of it. This means that all or some aspects may not be subject to certain trade agreement obligations.

You may refer to Canada's Market Access Schedule for Canada’s set-asides. For examples, see:

CFTA : Government Procurement Chapter, “Set-asides”

WTO-AGP : Annex 7 – General Notes

CETA : Annex 19-7

CPTPP : Annex 15-A , Section G

Indigenous Peoples

All trade agreements contain mechanisms that allow Canada to add measures, including but not limited to set-asides, for the benefit of Indigenous Peoples or businesses in a procurement. This includes procurement obligations under Modern Treaties (Comprehensive Land Claims Agreements ).

If a measure is adopted or maintained for Indigenous Peoples in a procurement that is otherwise covered by trade agreements, some or all aspects of it may not be subject to trade agreement obligations.

Coverage of dredging services under the trade agreements

The coverage of dredging services is described below.

Applicable trade agreements

The CETA and the Canada-UK TCA are the only international trade agreements that cover dredging services (see CETA Annex 19-6 - Construction services and Canada-UK TCA Article I. As such, when CETA and the Canada-UK TCA apply, in addition to dredging equipment of Canadian make and manufacture, dredging equipment and vessels of European Union (EU) member state and United Kingdom (UK) make and manufacture are permitted on the work, whether in dredging procurements or construction services procurements with dredging as a component.

Conditions for coverage

Dredging services incidental to construction services contracts are only covered for central government entities. The CETA and Canada-UK TCA construction services thresholds for central government entities apply.

For dredging procurements under CETA and the Canada-UK TCA , or dredging services incidental to CETA and Canada-UK TCA covered construction services contracts, one of the following requirements apply:

equipment and vessels must be of Canadian, EU or UK make or manufacture

equipment and vessels must have been predominantly modified in Canada, the EU or the UK and has been owned by a person located in Canada, the EU or the UK for at least a year before offer submission

Vessel registration and licensing

The following requirements apply:

vessels used must be registered in Canada, the UK or an EU Member State

if not registered in Canada, the vessel must have a temporary licence under the Coasting Trade Act , granted based on specific non-discretionary requirements

the usual requirement, that a temporary licence is only issued if no Canadian vessel is available, does not apply in these cases

Applicability of trade agreements to standing offers and supply arrangements

You must assess the applicability of the trade agreements related to standing offers and supply arrangements. In your assessment, you must consider the following three factors:

whether the department for which the standing offer or supply arrangement is intended is covered under the trade agreements

whether the good or service categories involved are subject to the trade agreements

whether the total estimated value of all the call-ups (contracts) against an standing offer or all contracts under an supply arrangement meets or exceeds the trade agreement thresholds

If trade agreements are applicable, solicitations for standing offers and supply arrangements must comply with these agreements.

For standing offer, individual call-ups are not considered separate procurements under the trade agreements. They can proceed without following trade agreement procedures, provided that the procedures specified in the standing offer for making call-ups are followed.

For supply arrangements, each individual contract cannot be made under the supply arrangements without having considered trade agreement applicability and procedural obligations, whether at the RFSA stage or at the point of placing an individual contract. For more information refer to Request for supply arrangement .

Invoking a national security exception

The section below provides guidance on how to proceed when invoking a national security exception to exclude a procurement from one or more trade agreements.

Client responsibilities

When a client wants to invoke an NSE to exclude the procurement from a trade agreement on a PSPC Acquisitions Program (AP) requisition they must prepare a letter of request from the responsible Assistant Deputy Minister (ADM) or equivalent at their department to the ADM/AP for approval, regardless of dollar value, where PSPC/AP is the contracting authority.

This letter must explain the nature of the proposed procurement and, depending on the applicable trade agreement(s), how it relates to Canada’s:

“national security interests” or international obligations, the “maintenance of international peace and security,” CFTA (PDF), Article 801: National Security

“essential security interests for the procurement of arms, ammunition or war materials, or to procurement indispensable for national security or for national defence purposes” (see the relevant text in the trade agreements below):

CETA , Article 19.3 (1): Security and general exceptions

WTO-AGP , Article III (1): Security and General Exceptions

CPTPP , Article 29.2: Security Exceptions

Canada-Chile , Article Kbis-16 (1): Exceptions

Canada-Columbia , Article 1402 (1): Security and General Exceptions

Canada-Honduras , Article 17.3 (1): Security and General Exceptions

Canada-Korea , Article 22.2: National Security

Canada-Panama , Article 16.03 (1): Security and General Exceptions

Canada-Peru , Article 1402 (1): Security and General Exceptions

Canada-UK TCA , Article I

Canada-Ukraine (CUFTA) , Article 10.4 (1): Security and General Exceptions

First, the client should submit a draft of the NSE request letter to the PSPC Specialized Services for Procurement Group (SSPG) for review before it is signed by their responsible ADM or equivalent. The PSPC SSPG reviews all NSE requests before seeking PSPC ADM / AP approval. Then, the client submits the request to the PSPC ADM / AP responsible for approving NSE requests.

The ADM reviews the issue of invoking the NSE , not other procurement matters such as methods, plans or contract authority. Once the ADM makes a decision, they will inform the client in writing. For the approval process for NSE , you may refer to Approval authorities for Acquisition Program client's programs only .

Clients should collaborate with you to determine the appropriate procurement method, in parallel with any request for approval of an NSE . Even if an NSE is invoked, it is government policy to compete the requirement, unless exceptions to competitive contracting in the GCRs apply.

This may include posting an NPP or an Advance Contract Award Notice (ACAN) on the Tender opportunities page, unless security requirements prevent such actions.
